Domain Range Set notation




Exercise 7.7 Identify which expression represents a linear function.


a) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; |t| + 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; \dfrac{1}{t} + 4 \qquad \text{C:}\; 2t^2 + t + 1 \qquad \text{D:}\; -2t + 3\)


b) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; \dfrac{1}{y} + 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; |y| + 5 \qquad \text{C:}\; -2y - 5 \qquad \text{D:}\; 2y^2 - 3y + 3\)


c) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 2n - 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; 3n^2 - 2n + 4 \qquad \text{C:}\; \dfrac{1}{n} + 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; |n| + 3\)


d) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 2m^2 - 2m - 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; |m| + 3 \qquad \text{C:}\; \dfrac{1}{m} + 1 \qquad \text{D:}\; m + 3\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } D \text{ ‚ÄĒ } f(x) = -2t + 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } C \text{ ‚ÄĒ } f(x) = -2y - 5 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } A \text{ ‚ÄĒ } f(x) = 2n - 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } D \text{ ‚ÄĒ } f(x) = m + 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\]





Linear functions Slope y-intercept




Exercise 7.8 Evaluate the function at each given value.


a) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 2x - 3 \quad \text{Find: } f(-3), f(4), f(2)\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 3x - 6 \quad \text{Find: } f(0), f(-3), f(3)\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -x - 5 \quad \text{Find: } f(0), f(-1), f(2)\)


d) \(\displaystyle f(x) = x - 3 \quad \text{Find: } f(-1), f(2), f(1)\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(-3) = 2 \cdot (-3) - 3 = -9 \\[4pt] f(4) = 2 \cdot (4) - 3 = 5 \\[4pt] f(2) = 2 \cdot (2) - 3 = 1
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(0) = 3 \cdot (0) - 6 = -6 \\[4pt] f(-3) = 3 \cdot (-3) - 6 = -15 \\[4pt] f(3) = 3 \cdot (3) - 6 = 3
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(0) = -1 \cdot (0) - 5 = -5 \\[4pt] f(-1) = -1 \cdot (-1) - 5 = -4 \\[4pt] f(2) = -1 \cdot (2) - 5 = -7
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(-1) = 1 \cdot (-1) - 3 = -4 \\[4pt] f(2) = 1 \cdot (2) - 3 = -1 \\[4pt] f(1) = 1 \cdot (1) - 3 = -2
\end{aligned}
\]





Linear functions Slope y-intercept




Exercise 7.9 Find the slope of the line through each pair of points.


a) \(\displaystyle (3,\ 2) \text{ and } (1,\ -1)\)


b) \(\displaystyle (-3,\ 2) \text{ and } (-4,\ 5)\)


c) \(\displaystyle (2,\ 3) \text{ and } (6,\ -1)\)


d) \(\displaystyle (-3,\ -4) \text{ and } (-2,\ -2)\)


e) \(\displaystyle (-3,\ -1) \text{ and } (-5,\ -2)\)


f) \(\displaystyle (3,\ -2) \text{ and } (4,\ -4)\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-1 - (2)}{1 - (3)} = \dfrac{-3}{-2} = \dfrac{3}{2}
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{5 - (2)}{-4 - (-3)} = \dfrac{3}{-1} = -3
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-1 - (3)}{6 - (2)} = \dfrac{-4}{4} = -1
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-2 - (-4)}{-2 - (-3)} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2
\end{aligned}
\] e)
\[
\begin{aligned}
m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-2 - (-1)}{-5 - (-3)} = \dfrac{-1}{-2} = \dfrac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
\] f)
\[
\begin{aligned}
m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-4 - (-2)}{4 - (3)} = \dfrac{-2}{1} = -2
\end{aligned}
\]





Rate of change Slope formula Interpretation




Exercise 7.10 Find the rate of change from the table.


a) \(\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & 1 & 4 & 5 \\ \hline y & -1 & 5 & 11 & 13 \end{array}\)


b) \(\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & -1 & 3 & 5 \\ \hline y & 0 & -2 & -10 & -14 \end{array}\)


c) \(\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & 0 & 1 & 4 \\ \hline y & 4 & 2 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\)


d) \(\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -1 & 0 & 4 & 5 \\ \hline y & 0 & 2 & 10 & 12 \end{array}\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of change} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2 \\[4pt] \text{Constant rate ‚Üí linear, slope } m = 2
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of change} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{-2}{1} = -2 \\[4pt] \text{Constant rate ‚Üí linear, slope } m = -2
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of change} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{-2}{2} = -1 \\[4pt] \text{Constant rate ‚Üí linear, slope } m = -1
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of change} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2 \\[4pt] \text{Constant rate ‚Üí linear, slope } m = 2
\end{aligned}
\]





Rate of change Slope formula Interpretation




Exercise 7.11 Identify which expression represents a quadratic function.


a) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 3t^2 - 3t - 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; 3t^3 - 1t^2 + 3 \qquad \text{C:}\; 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; -2t + 4\)


b) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 2p^3 + 2p^2 + 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; -p^2 + 3p + 4 \qquad \text{C:}\; 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; -p + 4\)


c) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 3n^3 - 1n^2 + 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; -2n^2 + 4n - 2 \qquad \text{C:}\; -n - 1 \qquad \text{D:}\; 9\)


d) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; -3x + 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; -2x^2 + 2x \qquad \text{C:}\; 2x^3 x^2 + 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; 8\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A quadratic has highest degree 2.} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } A \text{ ‚ÄĒ } f(x) = 3t^2 - 3t - 3 \text{ has degree 2. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A quadratic has highest degree 2.} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } B \text{ ‚ÄĒ } f(x) = -p^2 + 3p + 4 \text{ has degree 2. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A quadratic has highest degree 2.} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } B \text{ ‚ÄĒ } f(x) = -2n^2 + 4n - 2 \text{ has degree 2. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A quadratic has highest degree 2.} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } B \text{ ‚ÄĒ } f(x) = -2x^2 + 2x \text{ has degree 2. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form




Exercise 7.12 Evaluate each quadratic function at the given values.


a) \(\displaystyle f(t) = -2t^2 - 3t - 2 \quad \text{Find: } f(-2), f(0), f(-1)\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(n) = 2n^2 - 2n - 4 \quad \text{Find: } f(0), f(-2), f(3)\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(n) = -n^2 - n - 3 \quad \text{Find: } f(2), f(1), f(-1)\)


d) \(\displaystyle f(m) = -m^2 - 2m - 2 \quad \text{Find: } f(-2), f(-1), f(0)\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(-2) = -2(-2)^2 + 6 - 2 = -4 \\[4pt] f(0) = -2(0)^2 + 0 - 2 = -2 \\[4pt] f(-1) = -2(-1)^2 + 3 - 2 = -1
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(0) = 2(0)^2 + 0 - 4 = -4 \\[4pt] f(-2) = 2(-2)^2 + 4 - 4 = 8 \\[4pt] f(3) = 2(3)^2 - 6 - 4 = 8
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(2) = -1(2)^2 - 2 - 3 = -9 \\[4pt] f(1) = -1(1)^2 - 1 - 3 = -5 \\[4pt] f(-1) = -1(-1)^2 + 1 - 3 = -3
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(-2) = -1(-2)^2 + 4 - 2 = -2 \\[4pt] f(-1) = -1(-1)^2 + 2 - 2 = -1 \\[4pt] f(0) = -1(0)^2 + 0 - 2 = -2
\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form

Function definition Domain & range Arrow diagrams

Exercise 7.15 Read and interpret the linear function.
A phone plan costs $30 per month plus $3 per GB of data.
(a) Write an equation for \(T\) in terms of \(g\).
(b) Interpret the slope and intercept in context.
(c) Find the value when \(g = 5\).






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) } T = 3g + 30 \\[4pt] \text{(b) Slope } = 3: \text{ cost per GB used} \\[4pt] \text{Intercept } = 30: \text{ monthly base cost} \\[4pt] \text{(c) When } g = 5: T = 3(5) + 30 = 45 \text{ \$}
\end{aligned}
\]





Linear functions Slope y-intercept




Exercise 7.16 Interpret the slope in context.
A plumber charges a call-out fee of $39 plus $2 per hour. The equation is \(C = 2h + 39\).
(a) Identify the slope and interpret it in context.
(b) What does the intercept represent?






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Slope } = 2 \text{ (C per hour)} \\[4pt] \text{cost per hour of work} \\[4pt] \text{(b) Intercept } = 39: \text{ fixed call-out fee}
\end{aligned}
\]





Rate of change Slope formula Interpretation




Exercise 7.17 Find intercepts and analyse the graph.
Consider the linear function \(y = -2x + 1\).

                               x y  -4  -3  -2  -1  1  2  3  4   (-4,9)  (0,1)

(a) Find the \(x\)-intercept and \(y\)-intercept.
(b) Use the graph to verify your intercepts.






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
y\text{-int: set }x=0: y = 1 \\[4pt] x\text{-int: set }y=0: 0 = -2x + 1 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt] \text{Plot the points: x-int at }(\dfrac{1}{2},\, 0)\text{ and y-int at }(0,\ 1).
\end{aligned}
\]





Graphing Intercepts Parallel & perpendicular




Exercise 7.18 Write the equation of the line.
A line passes through \((1,\ -7)\) and \((0,\ -5)\).

                               x y  -4  -3  -2  -1  1  2  3  4   (-4,3)  (0,-5)

(a) Calculate the slope of the line.
(b) Write the equation in the form \(y = mx + c\).






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
m = \dfrac{-5 - (-7)}{0 - (1)} = -2 \\[4pt] y - (-7) = -2(x - (1)) \\[4pt] y = -2x - 5
\end{aligned}
\]

Exercise 7.19 Analyse the parabola.
The parabola is given by \(f(x) = 2(x - 3)^2 - 1\).

                                                                   x y   x=3  (3, -1)  2.3  3.7

(a) State the vertex and axis of symmetry.
(b) Does the parabola open upward or downward? Why?
(c) Write the equation in standard form \(f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c\).






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Vertex: } (3,\ -1), \text{ axis: } x = 3 \\[4pt] \text{(b) Opens upward since } a = 2 \text{{ > 0}} \\[4pt] \text{(c) Standard form: } f(x) = 2x^2 - 12x + 17
\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form




Exercise 7.20 Convert from vertex form to standard form.


a) \(\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 2)^2 + 6\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -2(x - 2)^2 - 6\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 2(x + 1)^2 - 3\)


d) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 2(x - 1)^2 - 2\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expand the bracket:} \\[4pt] = 1(x^2 + 4x + 4) + 6 \\[4pt] = x^2 + 4x + 10
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expand the bracket:} \\[4pt] = -2(x^2 - 4x + 4) - 6 \\[4pt] = -2x^2 + 8x - 14
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expand the bracket:} \\[4pt] = 2(x^2 + 2x + 1) - 3 \\[4pt] = 2x^2 + 4x - 1
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expand the bracket:} \\[4pt] = 2(x^2 - 2x + 1) - 2 \\[4pt] = 2x^2 - 4x
\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form




Exercise 7.21 Expand to standard form.


a) \(\displaystyle f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 5)\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -(x - 1)(x - 5)\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 1)(x + 4)\)


d) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 2(x + 5)(x - 4)\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expand} \\[4pt] = 1(x^2 - 6x + 5) \\[4pt] = x^2 - 6x + 5
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expand} \\[4pt] = -1(x^2 - 6x + 5) \\[4pt] = -x^2 + 6x - 5
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expand} \\[4pt] = 1(x^2 + 5x + 4) \\[4pt] = x^2 + 5x + 4
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}

\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form




Exercise 7.22 Find the vertex of each quadratic function.


a) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 3x^2 + 6x + 2\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 2x^2 + 12x + 22\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 2x + 5\)


d) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -2x^2 + 2\)


e) \(\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3\)


f) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 2x^2 + 2\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(6)}{2(3)} = -1 \\[4pt] y = 3(-1)^2 - 6 + 2 = -1 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=-1,\ y=-1
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(12)}{2(2)} = -3 \\[4pt] y = 2(-3)^2 - 36 + 22 = 4 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=-3,\ y=4
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(-2)}{2(1)} = 1 \\[4pt] y = 1(1)^2 - 2 + 5 = 4 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=1,\ y=4
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(0)}{2(-2)} = 0 \\[4pt] y = -2(0)^2  + 2 = 2 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=0,\ y=2
\end{aligned}
\] e)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(-4)}{2(1)} = 2 \\[4pt] y = 1(2)^2 - 8 + 3 = -1 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=2,\ y=-1
\end{aligned}
\] f)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(0)}{2(2)} = 0 \\[4pt] y = 2(0)^2  + 2 = 2 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=0,\ y=2
\end{aligned}
\]

c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -(x + 3)^2 - 1\)

TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[

\[

\] c)
\[

\] d)
\[

\]

TipūüĒć View Solution

Domain & range Vertex Quadratic functions




Exercise 7.26 Read the graph and find the equation.

                                                                   x y 

(a) State the roots (x-intercepts) of the function.

TipūüĒć View Solution

\]





Vertex Axis of symmetry Roots

Exercise 7.27 Find the equation of each line described.


a) \(\displaystyle \text{Parallel to } y = 3x + 3\text{, through } (-2,\ -3)\)


b) \(\displaystyle \text{Parallel to } y = 3x - 4\text{, through } (3,\ -2)\)


c) \(\displaystyle \text{Parallel to } y = -2x + 4\text{, through } (-3,\ 2)\)


d) \(\displaystyle \text{Parallel to } y = -x + 1\text{, through } (-3,\ 1)\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parallel lines have equal slopes: } m = 3 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(-2,\ -3): -3 = 3(-2) + c \\[4pt] c = 3 \\[4pt] y = 3x + 3
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parallel lines have equal slopes: } m = 3 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(3,\ -2): -2 = 3(3) + c \\[4pt] c = -11 \\[4pt] y = 3x - 11
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parallel lines have equal slopes: } m = -2 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(-3,\ 2): 2 = -2(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = -4 \\[4pt] y = -2x - 4
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parallel lines have equal slopes: } m = -1 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(-3,\ 1): 1 = -1(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = -2 \\[4pt] y = -x - 2
\end{aligned}
\]





Graphing Intercepts Parallel & perpendicular




Exercise 7.28 Find the equation of each line described.


a) \(\displaystyle \text{Perpendicular to } y = -x - 4\text{, through } (2,\ -2)\)


b) \(\displaystyle \text{Perpendicular to } y = 2x + 4\text{, through } (0,\ 0)\)


c) \(\displaystyle \text{Perpendicular to } y = -x + 1\text{, through } (-1,\ 0)\)


d) \(\displaystyle \text{Perpendicular to } y = 2x - 2\text{, through } (0,\ 1)\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Perpendicular slope: } m_\perp = \dfrac{-1}{-1} = 1 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(2,\ -2): -2 = 1(2) + c \\[4pt] c = -4 \\[4pt] y = x - 4
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Perpendicular slope: } m_\perp = \dfrac{-1}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt] \text{Through }(0,\ 0): 0 = -\dfrac{1}{2}(0) + c \\[4pt] c = 0 \\[4pt] y = -\dfrac{1}{2}x
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Perpendicular slope: } m_\perp = \dfrac{-1}{-1} = 1 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(-1,\ 0): 0 = 1(-1) + c \\[4pt] c = 1 \\[4pt] y = x + 1
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Perpendicular slope: } m_\perp = \dfrac{-1}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt] \text{Through }(0,\ 1): 1 = -\dfrac{1}{2}(0) + c \\[4pt] c = 1 \\[4pt] y = -\dfrac{1}{2}x + 1
\end{aligned}
\]





Graphing Intercepts Parallel & perpendicular




Exercise 7.29 Form and solve a linear model.
A phone plan charges $10 per month plus $2 per GB of data.
(a) Write an equation for total monthly bill \(T\) after using \(g\) GB.
(b) Find the bill for 12 GB usage.
(c) How many GB can be used for $90?






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) } T = 2g + 10 \\[4pt] \text{(b) } T = 2(12) + 10 = 34 \text{ \$} \\[4pt] \text{(c) } 90 = 2g + 10 \Rightarrow g = 40 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
\]





Linear modelling Real-world context Interpretation




Exercise 7.30 Convert from vertex form to factored form.


a) \(\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 1)^2 - 1\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 4\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 3)^2 - 4\)


d) \(\displaystyle f(x) = (x - 4)^2 - 4\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Set } f(x) = 0: \\[4pt] (x - (-1))^2 = 1 \\[4pt] x + 1 = \pm 1 \\[4pt] x = -2 \text{ or } x = 0 \\[4pt] \therefore f(x) = (x + 2)(x)
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Set } f(x) = 0: \\[4pt] (x - (2))^2 = 4 \\[4pt] x - 2 = \pm 2 \\[4pt] x = 0 \text{ or } x = 4 \\[4pt] \therefore f(x) = (x)(x - 4)
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Set } f(x) = 0: \\[4pt] (x - (-3))^2 = 4 \\[4pt] x + 3 = \pm 2 \\[4pt] x = -5 \text{ or } x = -1 \\[4pt] \therefore f(x) = (x + 5)(x + 1)
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Set } f(x) = 0: \\[4pt] (x - (4))^2 = 4 \\[4pt] x - 4 = \pm 2 \\[4pt] x = 2 \text{ or } x = 6 \\[4pt] \therefore f(x) = (x - 2)(x - 6)
\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form




Exercise 7.31 Convert to vertex form by completing the square.


a) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -x^2 + 2x - 7\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 4\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -x^2 - 6\)


d) \(\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3\)








TipūüĒć View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Factor out } -1\text{:} \\[4pt] = -1\left(x^2 - 2x\right) - 7 \\[4pt] = -1\left(x - 1\right)^2 + 1 - 7 \\[4pt] = -(x - 1)^2 - 6
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Complete the square:} \\[4pt] = (x + 0)^2 - 0 - 4 \\[4pt] = (x + 0)^2 - 4 \\[4pt] = (x)^2 - 4
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Factor out } -1\text{:} \\[4pt] = -1\left(x^2 + 0x\right) - 6 \\[4pt] = -1\left(x + 0\right)^2 + 0 - 6 \\[4pt] = -(x)^2 - 6
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Complete the square:} \\[4pt] = (x - 2)^2 - 4 + 3 \\[4pt] = (x - 2)^2 - 1 \\[4pt] = (x - 2)^2 - 1
\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form




Exercise 7.32 Analyse the projectile model.
A ball is thrown upward. Its height (m) after \(t\) seconds is \(h(t) = -5t^2 + 15t\).

                                                                                                                                             t y   x=3/2  (3/2, 11)  0  3

(a) What is the initial height of the ball?
(b) Find the maximum height and when it occurs.
(c) Set up the equation to find when the ball hits the ground.






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) } h(0) = 0 \text{ m ‚ÄĒ initial height} \\[4pt] \text{(b) Vertex: } t = \dfrac{-15}{2(-5)} = \dfrac{3}{2} \text{ s} \\[4pt] \text{Max height } = 11 \text{ m} \\[4pt] \text{(c) Set } h = 0: -5t^2 + 15t = 0
\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic modelling Projectile motion Revenue




Exercise 7.33 Analyse the revenue model.
A company sells items at price \(p\) dollars. Demand is \(100 - 2p\) units.
(a) Write an equation for revenue \(R\) in terms of \(p\).
(b) Find the price that maximises revenue and state the maximum revenue.






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Demand } = 100 - 2p \\[4pt] R = p(100 - 2p) = 100p - 2p^2 \\[4pt] \text{(b) Vertex: } p = \dfrac{100}{2 \times 2} = 25 \\[4pt] R_{\text{max}} = 1250
\end{aligned}
\]





Quadratic modelling Projectile motion Revenue




Exercise 7.34 Find the intersection of the two functions.
\(f(x) = x^2 - 9x + 6\) and \(g(x) = -2x - 4\)
(a) Set \(f(x) = g(x)\) and form a quadratic equation.
(b) Solve to find the \(x\)-coordinates of the intersection points.
(c) State the coordinates of the intersection points.






TipūüĒć View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(a)} \quad x^2 - 9x + 6 = -2x - 4 \implies x^2 - 7x + 10 = 0 \\[6pt] \textbf{(b)} \quad (x - 2)(x - 5) = 0 \implies x = 2 \text{ or } x = 5 \\[6pt] \textbf{(c)} \quad (2,\ -8) \text{ and } (5,\ -14)
\end{aligned}
\]

Identify and correctly label the hypotenuse, opposite side, and adjacent side relative to a given angle in a right-angled triangle.


Hypotenuse Opposite & adjacent Angle reference




LT2


Recall the Trigonometric Ratios


Define and recall sin őł, cos őł, and tan őł as ratios of sides using SOH-CAH-TOA, and identify the correct ratio for a given problem.


SOH-CAH-TOA Ratio selection Fraction form




LT3


Find a Missing Side Length


Use a trigonometric ratio to set up and solve an equation to find an unknown side length, given one side and one acute angle.


Equation set-up Multiply & divide Calculator use




LT4


Find a Missing Angle


Use inverse trigonometric functions (sin‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, cos‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, tan‚ĀĽ¬Ļ) to find an unknown angle when two sides of a right-angled triangle are known.


Inverse trig Two-side ratio Degree rounding




LT5


Solve Elevation & Depression Problems


Interpret diagrams and written descriptions involving angles of elevation and depression, and apply trigonometry to find distances and heights.


Elevation & depression Diagram reading Real-world modelling




LT6


Solve Multi-step Problems


Combine two or more trigonometric steps, or integrate trigonometry with other geometric knowledge, to solve complex problems.


Multi-step reasoning Combined geometry Exact & rounded answers

title: 8¬† Unit 1: Trigonometry
text:
8.2 ūüóāÔłŹ Formula Card

Trigonometry ‚ÄĒ Right-Angled Triangles


Labelling the Triangle

ūüí° Key Rule The labels opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse depend on which angle őł you are working from. The hypotenuse is always opposite the right angle ‚ÄĒ it never changes.


Three sides relative to angle őł

  
  
  
  
  
  
  őł
  
  Opposite
  Adjacent
  Hypotenuse


Hypotenuse (H) ‚ÄĒ longest side, always opposite the right angle
Opposite (O) ‚ÄĒ the side directly across from angle őł
Adjacent (A) ‚ÄĒ the side next to őł (not the hypotenuse)





SOH-CAH-TOA

ūüéĮ Memory Trick ‚ÄĒ Some Old Houses Can Always Hide Their Old Age


The three ratios
\[\sin\theta = \frac{O}{H} \qquad \cos\theta = \frac{A}{H} \qquad \tan\theta = \frac{O}{A}\]
Choosing the right ratio ‚ÄĒ identify which two sides are involved (known + unknown), then pick the ratio that contains both of them.



Known & Unknown
Ratio to use




Opp and Hyp
sin


Adj and Hyp
cos


Opp and Adj
tan







Finding a Missing Side

‚ö†ÔłŹ Common Mistake Check whether the unknown is on the top or bottom before rearranging. \(x = H \times \sin\theta\) is very different from \(x = \dfrac{O}{\sin\theta}\).


Unknown on top ‚ÄĒ multiply
\[\sin\theta = \frac{x}{H} \implies x = H \times \sin\theta\]
\[\cos\theta = \frac{x}{H} \implies x = H \times \cos\theta\]
\[\tan\theta = \frac{x}{A} \implies x = A \times \tan\theta\]
Unknown on bottom ‚ÄĒ divide
\[\sin\theta = \frac{O}{x} \implies x = \frac{O}{\sin\theta}\]
\[\cos\theta = \frac{A}{x} \implies x = \frac{A}{\cos\theta}\]
\[\tan\theta = \frac{O}{x} \implies x = \frac{O}{\tan\theta}\]
Example ‚ÄĒ find \(x\) given hypotenuse = 10 cm, őł = 30¬į: \[\sin 30¬į = \frac{x}{10} \implies x = 10 \times \sin 30¬į = 5 \text{ cm}\]




Finding a Missing Angle

ūüďĚ Key Idea Use the inverse trig function to undo the ratio. On your calculator: press SHIFT (or 2nd) then sin / cos / tan.


The three inverse functions
\[\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{O}{H}\right)\]
\[\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{A}{H}\right)\]
\[\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{O}{A}\right)\]
Example ‚ÄĒ find őł given opposite = 5 cm, hypotenuse = 13 cm: \[\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13} \implies \theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{5}{13}\right) \approx 22.6¬į\]




Angles of Elevation & Depression

ūüí° Key Fact Both angles are measured from the horizontal. They are equal (alternate angles) when the ground is level.


Elevation ‚ÄĒ angle measured upward from horizontal

  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  őł
  
  Observer
  h
  d

\[\tan\theta = \frac{h}{d} \qquad h = d\tan\theta \qquad d = \frac{h}{\tan\theta}\]
Depression ‚ÄĒ angle measured downward from horizontal

  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  őł
  
  h
  d
  Boat

\[\tan\theta = \frac{h}{d} \qquad \text{(same formula ‚ÄĒ alternate angles)}\]




Angle Sum Reminder

In any right-angled triangle the two acute angles sum to 90¬į: \[\alpha + \beta = 90¬į \implies \beta = 90¬į - \alpha\]

Exercise 8.1 Label the hypotenuse, opposite, and adjacent sides relative to the marked angle.
Triangle \(UDA\) has a right angle at \(U\) and angle \(\theta\) at \(D\).

 U D A   őł Not drawn to scale

(a) Which side is the hypotenuse?
(b) Which side is opposite to angle őł?
(c) Which side is adjacent to angle őł?






TipūüĒć View Solution






 U D A Adj Hyp Opp   őł Not drawn to scale

a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hyp = DA (opposite right angle at U)}
\end{aligned}
\]
b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Opp = UA (across from } \theta \text{ at D)}
\end{aligned}
\]
c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Adj = UD (next to } \theta \text{, not hyp)}
\end{aligned}
\]





Hypotenuse identification Opposite side Adjacent side

 8 11.31 8   45¬į Not drawn to scale

a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta = \dfrac{\text{Opp}}{\text{Hyp}} = \dfrac{8}{11.31}
\end{aligned}
\]

Exercise 8.16 Show all working. Give your answer to 2 decimal places.
A hill is inclined at 12¬į to the horizontal, base at sea level.
(a) If you walk 150 m up the hill, what is your height above sea level?
(b) If you climb to 80 m above sea level, how far have you walked?

\[

\end{aligned}
\]
b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sin 12¬į = \dfrac{80}{d} \\ d = \dfrac{80}{\sin 12¬į} = 384.78 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
\]

A 65 m building. Point \(A\) is 113 m from its base.

Given: = 10 ¬∑ = 55¬į ¬∑ = 85

 10 h   55¬į Not drawn to scale

 10 8.2   55¬į Not drawn to scale

\end{aligned}
\]





sin ratio Height calculation Unit conversion




Exercise 8.20 Show all working. Give angles to 1 decimal place.
A ladder leans against a wall. Wall height = 5.6 m, base distance = 0.9 m. Safe angle: 70¬į‚Äď80¬į to the ground.

 0.9m L 5.6m   őł Not drawn to scale

(b) Calculate the length of the ladder.






TipūüĒć View Solution






 0.9m 5.67m 5.6m   80.9¬į Not drawn to scale

a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta = \dfrac{5.6}{0.9} \\ \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{5.6}{0.9}\right) = 80.9¬į \\ 80.9¬į \text{ is NOT safe (outside 70¬į‚Äď80¬į)}
\end{aligned}
\]
b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
L^2 = 5.6^2 + 0.9^2 = 32.17 \\ L = \sqrt{32.17} = 5.67 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
\]

Two points A and B on opposite sides of a tower. From A: elevation 30¬į, distance 15 m. From B: elevation 35¬į.

Exercise 8.26 Show all working clearly. Give your answer to 2 decimal places.
Two right-angled triangles share side BD.
Given: AB=8 ¬∑ BC=2 ¬∑ BCD=55¬į
(a) Calculate angle \(ABD\) (marked \(x\)).






TipūüĒć View Solution





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\triangle BCD: \quad BD = BC \times \tan 55¬į = 2 \times \tan 55¬į = 2.86 \text{ cm} \\ \triangle ABD: \quad \sin(x) = \dfrac{2.86}{8} \\ x = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2.86}{8}\right) = 20.9¬į
\end{aligned}
\]





Multi-step reasoning Vertex labelling Combined trig




Exercise 8.27 Show all working clearly. Give your answer to 2 decimal places.
Right-angled triangles ABD and BCD share side BD.
Given: AB=16 ¬∑ ABD=70¬į ¬∑ ABD:DBC=5:2
(a) Work out the length of \(CD\).






TipūüĒć View Solution





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\angle DBC = \dfrac{2}{5} \times 70¬į = 28.0¬į \\ BD = 16 \sin 70¬į = 15.04 \text{ cm} \\ CD = BD \times \tan 28.0¬į = 15.04 \times \tan 28.0¬į = 8 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
\]





Ratio splitting Multi-step trig Angle arithmetic




Exercise 8.28 Show all working. Give your answer to 1 decimal place.
Isosceles triangle \(FLG\): \(FL = LG = 32\) cm, \(FG = 20\) cm.

 F G L 20 32  32  Not drawn to scale

(a) Work out angle \(LFG\).
(b) Work out angle \(FLG\).






TipūüĒć View Solution






 F G L 20 32  32   18.2¬į  18.2¬į  143.6¬į Not drawn to scale

a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Drop perpendicular from L to midpoint M of FG} \\ FM = \dfrac{20}{2} = 10.0 \text{ cm} \\ \sin(\angle LFG) = \dfrac{10.0}{32} \\ \angle LFG = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{10.0}{32}\right) = 18.2¬į
\end{aligned}
\]
b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\angle FLG = 180¬į - 2 \times 18.2¬į = 143.6¬į
\end{aligned}
\]





Isosceles properties Perpendicular bisector sin application




Exercise 8.29 Show all working. Give bearing to nearest degree.
A helicopter flies 10 km due east, then 6 km due north.
(a) Find the direct distance.
(b) Calculate the bearing.






TipūüĒć View Solution





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
d^2 = 10^2 + 6^2 = 136 \\ d = \sqrt{136} = 11.66 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
\]
b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta = \dfrac{10}{6} \\ \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{10}{6}\right) = 59.0¬į \\ \text{Bearing} = 059¬į
\end{aligned}
\]





Pythagoras tan‚ĀĽ¬Ļ for bearing Compass directions




Exercise 8.30 Show all working. Give your answer to 2 decimal places.
A boat approaches a 120 m cliff with a 25 m lighthouse on top. Angle of elevation to top of lighthouse is 20¬į.
Given: = 120 ¬∑ = 25 ¬∑ = 20¬į
(a) Find the distance from the boat to the base of the cliff.
(b) Find the angle of elevation from the boat to the top of the cliff.






TipūüĒć View Solution





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan 20¬į = \dfrac{145}{d} \\ d = \dfrac{145}{\tan 20¬į} = 398.38 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
\]
b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tan\alpha = \dfrac{120}{398.38} \\ \alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{120}{398.38}\right) = 16.8¬į
\end{aligned}
\]





Multi-step trig Total height Elevation angles




Exercise 8.31 Show all working. Give your answer to 2 decimal places.
A chord of 5.3 cm subtends an angle of 34¬į at the centre.
Given: = 5.3 ¬∑ = 34¬į
(a) Find the radius of the circle.






TipūüĒć View Solution





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Half-chord} = \dfrac{5.3}{2} = 2.65 \text{ cm} \\ \sin 34¬į = \dfrac{2.65}{r} \\ r = \dfrac{2.65}{\sin 34¬į} = 4.74 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
\]





Circle geometry sin ratio Right triangle in circle




Exercise 8.32 Show all working. Give your answer to 2 decimal places.
From 200 m away: elevation to flagpole bottom = 34¬į, to top = 38¬į.
(a) Find the height of the flagpole.






TipūüĒć View Solution





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
h_1 = 200 \tan 34¬į = 134.9 \text{ m} \\ h_2 = 200 \tan 38¬į = 156.26 \text{ m} \\ \text{Flagpole} = 156.26 - 134.9 = 21.36 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
\]





Multi-step trig Two-angle setup Subtraction




Exercise 8.33 Show all working. Give your answer to 2 decimal places.
Depression from top of 18 m cliff: first 3.3¬į, then 5.5¬į. How far does the boat move?
(a) How much closer must the boat move?






TipūüĒć View Solution





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
d_1 = \dfrac{18}{\tan 3.3¬į} = 312.18 \text{ m} \\ d_2 = \dfrac{18}{\tan 5.5¬į} = 186.94 \text{ m} \\ \text{Moved} = 312.18 - 186.94 = 125.24 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
\]





Two depression angles Distance difference Multi-step trig




Exercise 8.34 Show all working. Give your answer to 2 decimal places.
Helicopter at 90 km/h. Takes 15 s to go from overhead to elevation angle 65¬į.
(a) Find the height of the helicopter.






TipūüĒć View Solution





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance} = 90 \times \dfrac{15}{3600} = 0.38 \text{ km} \\ h = 0.38 \times \tan 65¬į = 0.81 \text{ km} \\ h = 810 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
\]
